title: Roto-Reflection Geometry of Pure Two-Qubit Entanglement
abstract: Pure two-qubit entanglement is usually characterized by scalar quantities such as concurrence. Here we show that it also has a natural geometric form. In the Pauli correlation tensor, maximally entangled states appear as improper orthogonal maps between two local Bloch spheres. These maps are roto-reflections. For partially entangled pure states, the same roto-reflection geometry is recovered after separating the contraction associated with concurrence. We call the corresponding geometric object the Entanglement Roto-Reflection Plane (ERRP). It organizes the maximally correlated directions of the two-qubit state and provides a covariant geometric complement to the scalar magnitude of entanglement.
summary: This paper develops the concept of the Entanglement Roto-Reflection Plane (ERRP) to geometrically characterize pure two-qubit entanglement. It shows that maximally entangled states exhibit correlation patterns described by roto-reflections within the Pauli correlation tensor framework. This geometric invariant provides a covariant complement to scalar measures like concurrence.
main_problem: To characterize pure two-qubit entanglement using a natural geometric form that goes beyond simple scalar measures like concurrence.
main_result: The authors introduce the Entanglement Roto-Reflection Plane (ERRP), a geometric object derived from the Pauli correlation tensor, which characterizes the correlations of any pure bipartite entangled state.
method: The analysis uses the Pauli decomposition of the density matrix to construct the correlation tensor $T$, and then extracts an improper orthogonal factor ($O$) that defines the ERRP geometry.
model_or_system:
The study focuses on pure two-qubit quantum states, analyzed using their Pauli correlation tensor $R_{\mu
u} = 	ext{Tr}(ho \sigma_\mu \otimes \sigma_
u)$. The framework is inherently mathematical and theoretical.
key_observables: Pauli correlation tensor ($T$), Concurrence ($C$), Entanglement Roto-Reflection Plane (ERRP) defined by an oriented normal ($\mathbf{n}$) and a signed angle ($\phi$).
important_parameters: The degree of entanglement, quantified by concurrence $C$, which dictates how the ERRP is factored out from the full correlation tensor.
main_assumptions: The construction is currently restricted to pure two-qubit states; extensions to mixed states require further decomposition techniques.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the Pauli decomposition visualization, showing local Bloch vectors and correlated triads. They also show maximally entangled states whose correlations are described by a roto-reflection geometry (e.g., in the xz-plane).
paper_structure: The paper establishes that entanglement has a geometric characterization beyond scalars. It derives this geometry using the Pauli tensor, showing how maximal entanglement corresponds to an improper orthogonal map (roto-reflection), and then generalizes this structure to partially entangled states by factoring out the concurrence contraction.
why_interesting: This work provides a novel, geometrically intuitive invariant for quantifying bipartite quantum correlations that is covariant under local unitaries, offering a powerful theoretical tool beyond standard entanglement measures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Arbitrary pure partially entangled two-qubit state. Inner spheres with reduced Bloch vectors represent the local reduced-state information while outer shells with correlated triads represent entangled part of the state. Arrows of the same color represent maximally correlated directions.

Figure 2: Maximally entangled state with ERRP equal to the xz-plane and a nonzero in-plane rotation. The state is obtained from |Φ+⟩by a local rotation I ⊗e−iασy/2 of the second qubit. The arc indicates the rotation angle of the roto-reflection.
Figure 3: Representation of an arbitrary pure partially entangled state where entanglement geometry is described through an ERRP.
